content:
# Joshua Chapter 7 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 230-231 | **Source Image:** `../../source-texts/hebrew-OT/JOS_007_page_001.jpg`

---

*[The Sin of Achan — Twenty-six verses that turn triumph to disaster in a single breath. Jericho has just fallen. The walls are rubble. And then one man reaches for what belongs to YHWH, and the entire nation breaks. This chapter is the dark mirror of chapter 6: the same God who brought down walls now withdraws his presence. Israel, unaware, marches against the tiny city of Ai — and runs. The narrative unfolds with the grim precision of a legal proceeding: crime, investigation, identification, confession, sentence, execution. PNEUMA renders this as what it is — a story about contagion, about how one hidden act poisons a whole people.]*

---

**1** But the sons of Israel broke faith with what had been set apart for destruction. Achan son of Carmi, son of Zabdi, son of Zerah, from the tribe of Judah, took some of what had been devoted to YHWH — and YHWH's anger blazed against all Israel.

**2** Joshua sent men from Jericho to Ai, near Beth-aven, east of Bethel, and told them, "Go up and scout the land." So the men went up and scouted Ai.

**3** They came back to Joshua and said, "Don't send the whole army up there. Two or three thousand men will be enough to take Ai. Don't exhaust the whole force for this — they're only a few people."

**4** So about three thousand men went up — and they fled before the men of Ai.

**5** The men of Ai struck down about thirty-six of them, chasing them from the city gate all the way to Shebarim, cutting them down on the slope. And the heart of the people melted and turned to water.

**6** Joshua tore his clothes and fell facedown on the ground before the ark of YHWH, staying there until evening — he and the elders of Israel — with dust on their heads.

**7** Joshua cried out, "Oh no, Lord YHWH! Why did you bring this people across the Jordan at all? Was it to hand us over to the Amorites, to destroy us? If only we had been willing to stay on the other side of the Jordan!

**8** "Please, Lord — what can I even say, now that Israel has turned its back and run from its enemies?

**9** "When the Canaanites and everyone else in the land hear about this, they will close in on us from every side and wipe our name from the earth. And then what will you do about your great name?"

**10** YHWH said to Joshua, "Get up! Why are you lying on your face?

**11** "Israel has sinned. They have violated my covenant — the one I commanded them to keep. They have taken from what was devoted to me. They have stolen. They have deceived. They have hidden it among their own belongings.

**12** "That is why the sons of Israel cannot stand against their enemies. That is why they turn and run — because they themselves have become contaminated by the devoted thing. I will not be with you anymore unless you remove what is devoted to destruction from your midst.

**13** "Get up. Consecrate the people and tell them: 'Make yourselves holy for tomorrow, because this is what YHWH, the God of Israel, says: Something set apart for destruction is hidden among you, Israel. You will not be able to stand against your enemies until you remove it from your midst.

**14** "'In the morning, you will come forward tribe by tribe. The tribe YHWH selects will come forward clan by clan. The clan YHWH selects will come forward family by family. The family YHWH selects will come forward man by man.

**15** "'The one found with what was devoted to destruction will be burned with fire — him and everything he has — because he violated the covenant of YHWH and committed an outrage in Israel.'"

**16** Joshua rose early the next morning and brought Israel forward tribe by tribe. The tribe of Judah was selected.

**17** He brought forward the clans of Judah, and the clan of the Zerahites was selected. He brought forward the clan of the Zerahites man by man, and Zabdi was selected.

**18** He brought forward Zabdi's household man by man — and Achan son of Carmi, son of Zabdi, son of Zerah, of the tribe of Judah, was taken.

**19** Joshua said to Achan, "My son, give honor to YHWH, the God of Israel, and make your confession to him. Tell me what you have done. Do not hide it from me."

**20** Achan answered Joshua, "It is true. I have sinned against YHWH, the God of Israel. This is what I did:

**21** "Among the plunder I saw a beautiful robe from Babylon, two hundred shekels of silver, and a bar of gold weighing fifty shekels. I wanted them. I took them. They are buried in the ground inside my tent, with the silver underneath."

**22** Joshua sent messengers, and they ran to the tent. And there it was — hidden in his tent, with the silver underneath.

**23** They took it all from the tent and brought it to Joshua and all the sons of Israel, and they spread it out before YHWH.

**24** Then Joshua — together with all Israel — took Achan son of Zerah, the silver, the robe, the bar of gold, his sons, his daughters, his ox, his donkey, his flock, his tent, and everything he had, and they brought them up to the Valley of Achor.

**25** Joshua said, "Why have you brought this disaster on us? YHWH will bring disaster on you today." Then all Israel stoned him, and they burned them with fire and pelted them with stones.

**26** They piled a great mound of stones over him — it is still there to this day. And YHWH turned from the ferocity of his anger. That is why the place has been called the Valley of Achor ever since.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**V. 1 — "Broke faith" (ma'al):** The Hebrew uses a cognate accusative, vayim'alu...ma'al — literally "they trespassed a trespass." The root ma'al is a sacral term: it does not mean ordinary wrongdoing but a specific breach of faith with what is holy. PNEUMA renders "broke faith with what had been set apart for destruction" to communicate both the relational betrayal and the sacred category. The Hebrew cherem (devoted thing) is the operative concept throughout the chapter — everything in Jericho was placed under a total ban, irrevocably belonging to YHWH. To take from the cherem was not theft from a city; it was theft from God.

**V. 1 — "YHWH's anger blazed against all Israel":** One man sinned; all Israel was charged. The Hebrew is explicit: the sons of Israel (plural) trespassed, even though only Achan acted. This corporate solidarity — the community bears the guilt of its members — is essential to the chapter's logic and to the entire theology of covenant in the Hebrew Bible. PNEUMA preserves "all Israel" to make the scope of the contamination clear.

**V. 1 — "Achan":** The Leningrad Codex reads 'Akhan. The LXX (Vaticanus) reads Achar, which is also the form in 1 Chronicles 2:7, where he is called "Achar, troubler of Israel." The variant Achar connects directly to the verb 'akhar (to trouble) in verse 25 and to the Valley of Achor ("Trouble"). PNEUMA follows the MT vocalization.

**V. 2 — "Ai":** The Hebrew consistently uses the definite article: ha-'Ai, "the Ruin." The city's name literally means "ruin" or "heap." PNEUMA follows English convention in dropping the article but notes that the original audience would have heard "the Ruin" every time the name appeared — an irony given that Israel is the one ruined in this chapter.

**V. 5 — "Shebarim":** This may be a place name or a common noun meaning "the broken places" or "the quarries" (from shavar, to break). The LXX apparently read it as a verb rather than a toponym, rendering "they crushed them." PNEUMA treats it as a place name, following the MT.

**V. 5 — "The heart of the people melted and turned to water":** This is a deliberate literary reversal. In Joshua 5:1, the hearts of the Canaanite kings "melted" when they heard of Israel's crossing. Now it is Israel whose heart melts. The same verb (masas) is used. The contagion of the cherem has transferred the enemies' terror to Israel itself. PNEUMA preserves the visceral image rather than abstracting it.

**V. 7 — Joshua's lament:** Joshua's prayer echoes the wilderness complaints of the Israelites — "why did you bring us out of Egypt to die?" (Exodus 14:11-12; Numbers 14:2-3). The interjection 'ahahh (rendered "Oh no") is raw anguish. The combination Adonai YHWH is the solemn address used in prophetic lament. Joshua sounds like a man whose faith has cracked. YHWH's response in verse 10 is correspondingly blunt: "Get up! Why are you lying on your face?" — the problem is not defeat but sin, and prayer without action is not the solution.

**V. 9 — "What will you do about your great name?":** Joshua pivots from despair to theology. His appeal to YHWH's reputation among the nations — "name theology" — has deep roots: Moses used the same argument (Exodus 32:12; Numbers 14:13-16; Deuteronomy 9:28). The logic is that YHWH's identity is publicly bound to Israel's fate. If Israel is destroyed, the nations will conclude that YHWH could not deliver. PNEUMA renders this as a direct, urgent question rather than a formal petition.

**V. 11 — The fivefold accusation:** YHWH's charge stacks five verbs connected by vegam ("and also"): sinned, transgressed the covenant, taken, stolen, deceived. The repetition of "and also" in Hebrew creates a relentless piling-on effect, as if YHWH is cataloguing each layer of the offense. PNEUMA breaks these into short, punching sentences to capture the rhetorical force in English, rather than preserving the "and also" chain that would sound artificial.

**V. 12 — "They themselves have become contaminated by the devoted thing":** This is the theological core of the chapter. The Hebrew says literally "they have become cherem" — Israel has become the very thing that was supposed to be destroyed. The cherem is contagious: touch it and you become it. PNEUMA renders "contaminated" to communicate the contagion concept, since English has no single word for "became a thing-under-the-ban-of-total-destruction."

**V. 15 — "An outrage" (nevalah):** This word does not mean "foolishness" in the modern sense. It designates morally repulsive conduct that tears at the fabric of the community — the same word used for the rape of Dinah (Genesis 34:7) and the atrocity at Gibeah (Judges 19-20). PNEUMA uses "outrage" to preserve the gravity.

**V. 19 — "Give honor to YHWH...make your confession":** The phrase "give glory to YHWH" is a solemn oath formula demanding truthful testimony — a courtroom adjuration in a sacred setting (cf. John 9:24 for the same formula in a later period). PNEUMA renders "give honor" and "make your confession" to convey the legal-religious solemnity: Achan is being formally charged before God and Israel to speak the truth.

**V. 21 — "A beautiful robe from Babylon":** The Hebrew says 'addereth Shin'ar — "a mantle of Shinar." Shinar is the biblical name for Babylonia (Genesis 10:10; 11:2). PNEUMA renders "Babylon" because that is what Shinar means to modern readers, and the dynamic equivalence principle favors communicating meaning over preserving an opaque geographical term. The "tongue of gold" (leshon zahav) — a bar or wedge shaped like a tongue — is rendered "bar of gold" for clarity. Achan's confession follows the pattern of the primordial fall: "I saw...I wanted...I took" (cf. Genesis 3:6: "she saw...it was desirable...she took"). The echo is unmistakable and almost certainly intentional.

**V. 24 — "His sons and his daughters":** The inclusion of Achan's family raises sharp questions about collective punishment. Deuteronomy 24:16 prohibits executing children for their fathers' sins. Several explanations have been offered: the family may have been complicit (the goods were hidden in the shared tent); the cherem contagion may have spread to all who shared the contaminated space; or this reflects the corporate household solidarity of ancient Israelite society. The text does not explicitly narrate the children's death — verse 25 uses the singular ("stoned him") before shifting to the plural ("burned them...stoned them"), which may distinguish Achan's execution from the destruction of his property.

**V. 25 — "Why have you brought this disaster on us? YHWH will bring disaster on you":** The Hebrew deploys a devastating wordplay: meh 'akhartanu ya'korkha YHWH — the root 'akhar (to trouble, to bring disaster) appears twice, binding Achan's crime to his punishment through the sound of the words. The same root gives the Valley of Achor its name and resonates with Achan's own name (especially in its variant form Achar). PNEUMA uses "brought this disaster...bring disaster" to preserve the mirror structure, though the sound-play on the name is inevitably lost in English.

**V. 25 — Singular then plural:** The MT shifts from "stoned him" (singular, Achan alone) to "burned them...stoned them" (plural, everything under the ban). This grammatical shift may distinguish the execution of Achan from the destruction of his possessions and livestock. The LXX reads plural throughout. PNEUMA follows the MT.

**V. 26 — "YHWH turned from the ferocity of his anger":** The chapter forms an envelope: it opens with YHWH's anger burning (v. 1) and closes with that anger extinguished (v. 26). The verb shuv (to turn) with charon 'af (burning of anger/nostril) is the standard expression for the lifting of divine wrath (cf. Exodus 32:12; Deuteronomy 13:17). The removal of the cherem restores the covenant. Hosea 2:17 later promises that the Valley of Achor will become "a doorway of hope" — a stunning eschatological reversal of this terrible scene.

**On the LXX (Vaticanus):** The Greek text of Joshua 7 contains several expansions and minor variants compared to the MT: an expanded genealogy in verse 1 (adding the father of Zerah), the reading Achar for Achan, the treatment of Shebarim as a verb in verse 5, the addition of "great" to the trespass in verse 1, and some rearrangement in the final execution verses. PNEUMA follows the Masoretic Text of the Leningrad Codex as its primary source throughout.

— PNEUMA
